editableNewDialogue.text: Quick wording check: which sounds smoother—“Browse curated templates” or “Browse a curated selection of templates”?
“Browse a curated selection of templates” reads smoother and a bit more natural. “Browse curated templates” is shorter and punchier, but slightly more abrupt.
Can you give three tagline options that feel modern and minimal?

1) “Modern templates. Ready to launch.”
2) “Clean design, built for clarity.”
3) “Curated layouts for a polished web presence.”
Random question: is it true honey never spoils?
Mostly yes—archaeologists have found edible honey in ancient tombs. Honey’s low moisture and acidity make it hard for microbes to grow, though it can crystallize over time.
And what’s the difference between a comet and an asteroid, in one sentence?
Asteroids are mostly rocky/metallic bodies, while comets contain more ice and dust and can form a visible coma and tail when they warm up near the Sun.
That rewording works—just make sure it fits the portfolio “Designs” page context where visitors are browsing recent templates and ongoing projects, so it reads like a quick intro above the gallery.
Got it—keeping it as a short, gallery-intro line for a portfolio Designs page showcasing recent templates and in-progress work.

Quick correction: it’s not that I dislike “Kenya” or “full-stack developer” in general. I only wanted those removed from that one branding description because the tone and context didn’t fit the page.

Got it—so the restriction isn’t global. You just meant: for that specific branding blurb, remove “Kenya” and the “full-stack developer” mention because it doesn’t suit the customer‑centric tone or the branding context.
Exactly. In other sections (like an About page), mentioning Kenya or being a full-stack developer can be totally fine.
